title: Hyperemesis Gravidarum
oneLiner: Severe, persistent nausea/vomiting in early pregnancy causing weight loss, dehydration, and electrolyte disturbances — risk of Wernicke’s encephalopathy if untreated.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Wernicke’s Encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency):** give thiamine *before* any IV glucose (dextrose). **Single finding:** nystagmus, ataxia, or confusion (any one = give IV thiamine immediately)
- **Electrolyte emergencies:** hypokalaemia (ECG: U waves, prolonged QT) → risk of arrhythmia; hyponatraemia (<125 mmol/L) → seizures. **Single finding:** ECG with prolonged QTc or U waves + known vomiting >7 days
- **Molar pregnancy / hyperemesis mimic:** severe vomiting + very high β-hCG + vaginal bleeding or uterine size > dates. **Single finding:** USS shows "snowstorm" pattern — do not assume benign vomiting
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5 things you’ll see):**
  - Persistent vomiting (>3/day) starting between 5–12 weeks gestation
  - Weight loss (>5% of pre-pregnancy weight)
  - Inability to keep down oral fluids/food
  - Dehydration signs: dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, postural BP drop
  - Ketonuria on urine dip
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Low jugular venous pressure (JVP), reduced skin turgor
  - Abdominal exam usually benign (no rebound, guarding) — exclude surgical causes
  - Neurological exam: check for nystagmus, ataxia, confusion (screen for Wernicke’s)
  - Uterine size + speculum exam if any bleeding to assess for molar pregnancy
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely → less likely:
  - **Gastroenteritis:** diarrhoea often present; vomiting precedes pregnancy symptoms
  - **GORD:** heartburn, regurgitation; no weight loss or ketonuria
  - **UTI:** dysuria, frequency; positive urine culture
  - **Pancreatitis:** epigastric pain radiating to back, raised lipase
  - **Molar pregnancy:** vaginal bleeding, uterine size > dates, very high β-hCG
  - **Thyrotoxicosis:** weight loss despite good appetite, tremor, tachycardia, palpable goitre
- **Splitter:** Dip urine for ketones and check serum K+/Na+ — **ketonuria + hypokalaemia/hyponatraemia with weight loss** strongly favours hyperemesis over other vomiting causes
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dip: ketones (≥1+ suggests dehydration), leucocytes/nitrites (UTI), pregnancy test (confirm gestation)
  - ECG: prolonged QTc, U waves (K+ depletion), flat T waves (Mg2+, K+)
  - Weigh patient (baseline) and calculate % weight loss
- **Bloods (panels + key markers):**
  - FBC (Hb, WCC, platelets)
  - U&Es (Na+, K+, urea, creatinine) — **K+ and Na+ are critical**
  - LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin) — often mildly elevated
  - Ca2+, Mg2+, PO4 (electrolyte depletion)
  - TFTs (TSH, free T4) — transient thyrotoxicosis is common; TSH low, fT4 raised but usually resolves spontaneously
  - β-hCG (if not already known; high levels suggest molar or multiple pregnancy)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** transvaginal ultrasound (8–12 week viability — exclude molar pregnancy, multiple pregnancy)
  - **Second-line:** if abdominal pain/suspicion of pancreatitis → lipase + abdominal USS (gallstones)
  - **Never needed:** CT/MRI routinely
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **PUQE Score** (Pregnancy-Unique Quantification of Emesis): score based on duration of nausea/vomiting, number of vomits per day, retching episodes. Score ≥13 = severe hyperemesis (aids monitoring but not diagnostic)
  - **Clinical diagnosis:** persistent vomiting + weight loss + ketonuria + electrolyte disturbance
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Serum K+ and Na+** — can drop rapidly; also **clinical neurological exam for Wernicke’s** (nystagmus, ataxia, confusion) — if any positive, give thiamine immediately
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - IV access (large bore if dehydrated)
  - **Give IV thiamine** (Pabrinex 2 pairs IV) **before any IV glucose/dextrose** — to prevent Wernicke’s
  - Start IV fluids: 0.9% saline + 20–40 mmol KCl (if K+ >3.0) at 125–200 mL/hr (adjust to urine output)
  - Antiemetic: cyclizine 50mg IV/IM or prochlorperazine 12.5mg IM (or [check local policy]) — avoid ondansetron until 10 weeks if possible (small risk of cleft palate)
  - Replace K+ aggressively: target K+ >3.5 (use central line if needed, [check local policy] for max rate)
  - Check ECG before starting potassium infusion
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue IV fluids with added KCl (check U&Es 4–6 hourly until stable)
  - Thiamine (Pabrinex 2 pairs IV BD or TDS for 3–5 days — [check local policy])
  - Oral antiemetic as step-down: prochlorperazine 5mg PO TDS, cyclizine 50mg PO TDS, or metoclopramide 10mg PO/IV TDS (avoid in first trimester if possible)
  - Consider oral steroid (prednisolone 40–50mg PO daily) if refractory (after 48h — [check local policy], require specialist review)
  - Monitor weight daily, strict fluid balance chart (aim urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr)
  - VTE prophylaxis: ensure hydration, encourage mobilisation; consider LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if admitted >24h and dehydration resolves — [check local policy]; weigh patient first
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue oral antiemetics for 2–4 weeks, then taper
  - Thiamine 100mg PO daily for duration of vomiting
  - Folic acid 5mg PO daily (higher dose than standard pregnancy supplement)
  - Dietetic referral: small frequent meals, high-carbohydrate, avoid trigger foods
  - Psychosocial support: anxiety/depression common; refer to perinatal mental health if needed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline + 20–40 mmol KCl per litre, rate 125–200 mL/hr (adjust to urine output) — do not use dextrose without thiamine
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (weight-adjusted — [check local policy])
  - **Diet/NBM:** start clear fluids → full diet as tolerated; if vomiting persists, keep NBM for 12–24h then reintroduce
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 2–4 hourly; strict fluid balance chart; daily weight; twice-daily urine dip for ketones
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Cyclizine: 50mg IV/IM/PO TDS (dose [check local policy] pregnancy safe)
  - Prochlorperazine: 12.5mg IM then 5mg PO TDS
  - Metoclopramide: 10mg IV/IM/PO TDS (caution: avoid in first trimester if possible; note dyskinetic reactions)
  - Ondansetron: 4–8mg PO/IV TDS (avoid during organogenesis weeks 4–10 if possible)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Domperidone: 10mg PO TDS (less used in UK due to QTc risk — [check local policy])
  - Prednisolone: 40–50mg PO OD for 3–5 days (refractory cases only, [check local policy])
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Cyclizine:** avoid in porphyria; anticholinergic side effects
  - **Metoclopramide:** avoid in GI obstruction, phaeochromocytoma; risk of acute dystonia (treat with procyclidine)
  - **Ondansetron:** caution if prolonged QTc; avoid in moderate/severe liver impairment
  - **Prochlorperazine:** avoid in Parkinson’s disease, bone marrow depression
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ondansetron: max 8mg daily in severe hepatic impairment ([check local policy])
  - Metoclopramide: reduce dose in severe renal impairment ([check local policy])
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving IV dextrose without prior thiamine** → precipitates Wernicke’s encephalopathy. **Always give Pabrinex before any glucose-containing fluid.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Wernicke’s encephalopathy (triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia)
  - Hypokalaemia → cardiac arrhythmia (prolonged QTc, torsades de pointes)
  - Mallory-Weiss tear (haematemesis from severe retching)
  - Acute kidney injury (pre-renal from volume depletion)
  - Thromboembolism (increased risk due to dehydration + prothrombotic state of pregnancy)
- **Chronic:**
  - Oesophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome — rare but life-threatening)
  - Nutritional deficiencies (B12, folate, iron, vitamin D)
  - Psychological: anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress, termination of pregnancy
  - Low birth weight / preterm delivery (if severe weight loss persists)
sections.Prognosis: - Recovery in >90% by 16–20 weeks; a minority continue until delivery. Recurrence rate ~15–20% in subsequent pregnancies. Maternal mortality is low if managed aggressively, but Wernicke’s can cause permanent brain damage. Risk of low birth weight increases if weight loss >10%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- ✅ **Always give IV thiamine before any dextrose** — write “thiamine STAT” immediately on admission
- ✅ **Monitor K+ and Mg2+ closely** — replete aggressively; low Mg2+ makes K+ replacement ineffective
- ✅ **Check TFTs** — transient thyrotoxicosis is common; avoid antithyroid drugs unless TSH <0.1 + fT4 markedly elevated + symptoms of thyrotoxicosis (tachycardia, tremor)
- ✅ **Screen for depression/anxiety** at every contact — hyperemesis can trigger perinatal mental health emergencies
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- ❌ **Giving IV dextrose (e.g. 5% dextrose alone or with fluids) without thiamine** — causes Wernicke’s
- ❌ **Under-replacing potassium** — aim K+ >3.5; ECG every 4–6 hours if K+ <3.0
- ❌ **Forgetting to check for ECG changes** — prolonged QTc can be silent; always do ECG if K+ <3.0 or Mg2+ <0.7
- ❌ **Prescribing ondansetron in first trimester without counselling** — small risk of cleft palate; use cyclizine or prochlorperazine first line
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Unable to keep down any fluids for >12 hours
  - Significantly worsened vomiting despite oral antiemetics
  - Any new confusion, double vision, unsteadiness, or fainting
  - Blood in vomit (fresh or coffee-ground) or severe epigastric pain
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP or early pregnancy assessment unit (EPAU) review in 2–3 days
  - Dietitian referral if weight loss >5%
  - Perinatal mental health assessment if mood persists low
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / obstetric reg if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Persistent vomiting despite 24h of IV fluids + antiemetics
  - New confusion, ataxia, or nystagmus (possible Wernicke’s)
  - Hypokalaemia <2.5 mmol/L or hyponatraemia <125 mmol/L
  - Any arrhythmia (ECG new AF, QTc >500ms)
  - Worsening renal function (creatinine >150 µmol/L)
  - Haematemesis (suspect Mallory-Weiss or oesophageal tear)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Seizures, reduced GCS, or refractory electrolyte disturbance requiring central line monitoring
  - Need for intubation due to severe vomiting or airway compromise

rawMarkdown:
## Hyperemesis Gravidarum
**One-liner:** Severe, persistent nausea/vomiting in early pregnancy causing weight loss, dehydration, and electrolyte disturbances — risk of Wernicke’s encephalopathy if untreated.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Wernicke’s Encephalopathy (thiamine deficiency):** give thiamine *before* any IV glucose (dextrose). **Single finding:** nystagmus, ataxia, or confusion (any one = give IV thiamine immediately)
- **Electrolyte emergencies:** hypokalaemia (ECG: U waves, prolonged QT) → risk of arrhythmia; hyponatraemia (<125 mmol/L) → seizures. **Single finding:** ECG with prolonged QTc or U waves + known vomiting >7 days
- **Molar pregnancy / hyperemesis mimic:** severe vomiting + very high β-hCG + vaginal bleeding or uterine size > dates. **Single finding:** USS shows "snowstorm" pattern — do not assume benign vomiting

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (3–5 things you’ll see):**
  - Persistent vomiting (>3/day) starting between 5–12 weeks gestation
  - Weight loss (>5% of pre-pregnancy weight)
  - Inability to keep down oral fluids/food
  - Dehydration signs: dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, postural BP drop
  - Ketonuria on urine dip
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Low jugular venous pressure (JVP), reduced skin turgor
  - Abdominal exam usually benign (no rebound, guarding) — exclude surgical causes
  - Neurological exam: check for nystagmus, ataxia, confusion (screen for Wernicke’s)
  - Uterine size + speculum exam if any bleeding to assess for molar pregnancy

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely → less likely:
  - **Gastroenteritis:** diarrhoea often present; vomiting precedes pregnancy symptoms
  - **GORD:** heartburn, regurgitation; no weight loss or ketonuria
  - **UTI:** dysuria, frequency; positive urine culture
  - **Pancreatitis:** epigastric pain radiating to back, raised lipase
  - **Molar pregnancy:** vaginal bleeding, uterine size > dates, very high β-hCG
  - **Thyrotoxicosis:** weight loss despite good appetite, tremor, tachycardia, palpable goitre
- **Splitter:** Dip urine for ketones and check serum K+/Na+ — **ketonuria + hypokalaemia/hyponatraemia with weight loss** strongly favours hyperemesis over other vomiting causes

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Urine dip: ketones (≥1+ suggests dehydration), leucocytes/nitrites (UTI), pregnancy test (confirm gestation)
  - ECG: prolonged QTc, U waves (K+ depletion), flat T waves (Mg2+, K+)
  - Weigh patient (baseline) and calculate % weight loss
- **Bloods (panels + key markers):**
  - FBC (Hb, WCC, platelets)
  - U&Es (Na+, K+, urea, creatinine) — **K+ and Na+ are critical**
  - LFTs (ALT, AST, bilirubin) — often mildly elevated
  - Ca2+, Mg2+, PO4 (electrolyte depletion)
  - TFTs (TSH, free T4) — transient thyrotoxicosis is common; TSH low, fT4 raised but usually resolves spontaneously
  - β-hCG (if not already known; high levels suggest molar or multiple pregnancy)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** transvaginal ultrasound (8–12 week viability — exclude molar pregnancy, multiple pregnancy)
  - **Second-line:** if abdominal pain/suspicion of pancreatitis → lipase + abdominal USS (gallstones)
  - **Never needed:** CT/MRI routinely
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **PUQE Score** (Pregnancy-Unique Quantification of Emesis): score based on duration of nausea/vomiting, number of vomits per day, retching episodes. Score ≥13 = severe hyperemesis (aids monitoring but not diagnostic)
  - **Clinical diagnosis:** persistent vomiting + weight loss + ketonuria + electrolyte disturbance
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Serum K+ and Na+** — can drop rapidly; also **clinical neurological exam for Wernicke’s** (nystagmus, ataxia, confusion) — if any positive, give thiamine immediately

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - IV access (large bore if dehydrated)
  - **Give IV thiamine** (Pabrinex 2 pairs IV) **before any IV glucose/dextrose** — to prevent Wernicke’s
  - Start IV fluids: 0.9% saline + 20–40 mmol KCl (if K+ >3.0) at 125–200 mL/hr (adjust to urine output)
  - Antiemetic: cyclizine 50mg IV/IM or prochlorperazine 12.5mg IM (or [check local policy]) — avoid ondansetron until 10 weeks if possible (small risk of cleft palate)
  - Replace K+ aggressively: target K+ >3.5 (use central line if needed, [check local policy] for max rate)
  - Check ECG before starting potassium infusion
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue IV fluids with added KCl (check U&Es 4–6 hourly until stable)
  - Thiamine (Pabrinex 2 pairs IV BD or TDS for 3–5 days — [check local policy])
  - Oral antiemetic as step-down: prochlorperazine 5mg PO TDS, cyclizine 50mg PO TDS, or metoclopramide 10mg PO/IV TDS (avoid in first trimester if possible)
  - Consider oral steroid (prednisolone 40–50mg PO daily) if refractory (after 48h — [check local policy], require specialist review)
  - Monitor weight daily, strict fluid balance chart (aim urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr)
  - VTE prophylaxis: ensure hydration, encourage mobilisation; consider LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) if admitted >24h and dehydration resolves — [check local policy]; weigh patient first
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Continue oral antiemetics for 2–4 weeks, then taper
  - Thiamine 100mg PO daily for duration of vomiting
  - Folic acid 5mg PO daily (higher dose than standard pregnancy supplement)
  - Dietetic referral: small frequent meals, high-carbohydrate, avoid trigger foods
  - Psychosocial support: anxiety/depression common; refer to perinatal mental health if needed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline + 20–40 mmol KCl per litre, rate 125–200 mL/hr (adjust to urine output) — do not use dextrose without thiamine
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40mg SC OD (weight-adjusted — [check local policy])
  - **Diet/NBM:** start clear fluids → full diet as tolerated; if vomiting persists, keep NBM for 12–24h then reintroduce
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 2–4 hourly; strict fluid balance chart; daily weight; twice-daily urine dip for ketones

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Cyclizine: 50mg IV/IM/PO TDS (dose [check local policy] pregnancy safe)
  - Prochlorperazine: 12.5mg IM then 5mg PO TDS
  - Metoclopramide: 10mg IV/IM/PO TDS (caution: avoid in first trimester if possible; note dyskinetic reactions)
  - Ondansetron: 4–8mg PO/IV TDS (avoid during organogenesis weeks 4–10 if possible)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Domperidone: 10mg PO TDS (less used in UK due to QTc risk — [check local policy])
  - Prednisolone: 40–50mg PO OD for 3–5 days (refractory cases only, [check local policy])
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - **Cyclizine:** avoid in porphyria; anticholinergic side effects
  - **Metoclopramide:** avoid in GI obstruction, phaeochromocytoma; risk of acute dystonia (treat with procyclidine)
  - **Ondansetron:** caution if prolonged QTc; avoid in moderate/severe liver impairment
  - **Prochlorperazine:** avoid in Parkinson’s disease, bone marrow depression
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ondansetron: max 8mg daily in severe hepatic impairment ([check local policy])
  - Metoclopramide: reduce dose in severe renal impairment ([check local policy])
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving IV dextrose without prior thiamine** → precipitates Wernicke’s encephalopathy. **Always give Pabrinex before any glucose-containing fluid.**

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Wernicke’s encephalopathy (triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia)
  - Hypokalaemia → cardiac arrhythmia (prolonged QTc, torsades de pointes)
  - Mallory-Weiss tear (haematemesis from severe retching)
  - Acute kidney injury (pre-renal from volume depletion)
  - Thromboembolism (increased risk due to dehydration + prothrombotic state of pregnancy)
- **Chronic:**
  - Oesophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome — rare but life-threatening)
  - Nutritional deficiencies (B12, folate, iron, vitamin D)
  - Psychological: anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress, termination of pregnancy
  - Low birth weight / preterm delivery (if severe weight loss persists)

### Prognosis
- Recovery in >90% by 16–20 weeks; a minority continue until delivery. Recurrence rate ~15–20% in subsequent pregnancies. Maternal mortality is low if managed aggressively, but Wernicke’s can cause permanent brain damage. Risk of low birth weight increases if weight loss >10%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- ✅ **Always give IV thiamine before any dextrose** — write “thiamine STAT” immediately on admission
- ✅ **Monitor K+ and Mg2+ closely** — replete aggressively; low Mg2+ makes K+ replacement ineffective
- ✅ **Check TFTs** — transient thyrotoxicosis is common; avoid antithyroid drugs unless TSH <0.1 + fT4 markedly elevated + symptoms of thyrotoxicosis (tachycardia, tremor)
- ✅ **Screen for depression/anxiety** at every contact — hyperemesis can trigger perinatal mental health emergencies

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- ❌ **Giving IV dextrose (e.g. 5% dextrose alone or with fluids) without thiamine** — causes Wernicke’s
- ❌ **Under-replacing potassium** — aim K+ >3.5; ECG every 4–6 hours if K+ <3.0
- ❌ **Forgetting to check for ECG changes** — prolonged QTc can be silent; always do ECG if K+ <3.0 or Mg2+ <0.7
- ❌ **Prescribing ondansetron in first trimester without counselling** — small risk of cleft palate; use cyclizine or prochlorperazine first line

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Unable to keep down any fluids for >12 hours
  - Significantly worsened vomiting despite oral antiemetics
  - Any new confusion, double vision, unsteadiness, or fainting
  - Blood in vomit (fresh or coffee-ground) or severe epigastric pain
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP or early pregnancy assessment unit (EPAU) review in 2–3 days
  - Dietitian referral if weight loss >5%
  - Perinatal mental health assessment if mood persists low

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / obstetric reg if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5
  - Persistent vomiting despite 24h of IV fluids + antiemetics
  - New confusion, ataxia, or nystagmus (possible Wernicke’s)
  - Hypokalaemia <2.5 mmol/L or hyponatraemia <125 mmol/L
  - Any arrhythmia (ECG new AF, QTc >500ms)
  - Worsening renal function (creatinine >150 µmol/L)
  - Haematemesis (suspect Mallory-Weiss or oesophageal tear)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Seizures, reduced GCS, or refractory electrolyte disturbance requiring central line monitoring
  - Need for intubation due to severe vomiting or airway compromise
